Day 0 
Preparations 
● Make sure that the tissue section being stained is not older than 6 months and has been stored at

● Set the oven’s temperature to 560C (double check the temperature using a thermometer).

documents.name: Xenium_reference_preparation.R
documents.excerpt:
#### Script to prepare single cell reference to be added to Xenium Web Panel Designer.

install.packages("BiocManager")

library(DropletUtils)
library(biomaRt)
library(Seurat)

sc_OC <- readRDS("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Results/escc_combined.rds")

#verify if values are integers or not (detect previous transformations)
all.equal(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x, as.intege

documents.name: Abstract HCA.docx
documents.excerpt: Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an increased cancer predisposition. Being fundamentally a DNA repair deficiency, FA makes patients hypersensitive to DNA damaging agents, most of which are used in conventional cancer therapies, urging the need for new treatments. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors us

document_index.title: Abstract HCA.docx
document_index.excerpt: Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an increased cancer predisposition. Being fundamentally a DNA repair deficiency, FA makes patients hypersensitive to DNA damaging agents, most of which are used in conventional cancer therapies, urging the need for new treatments. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors us

1 
 t-CyCIF protocol 
Day 0 
Preparations 
● Make sure that the tissue section being stained is not older than 6 months and has been stored at 
+40C. 
 
● Set the oven’s temperature to 560C (double check the temperature using a thermometer). 
o Takes 30 minutes to preheat and oven in Kauppi’s lab has been more reliable 
 
● Check the levels of xylene and alcohol in the dewaxing and rehydration series in the fume hood, if the 
levels are too low add more xylene or respective alcohol. Prepare fresh xylene and alcohol series after 
using them 5 times. 
o Refer to sticky notes in the hood for dilution volumes 
o If helpful, record each time you use the xylene and alcohol series 
 
● Make sure that all the buffers and antibodies are available: antigen retrieval (100mM citric acid pH 
6.0), odyssey blocking buffer, 30% H202, & 480mM NaOH, and 1x PBS. 
● Preparing citric acid buffer, 10 mM, pH 6.0: 
o From 100mM stock 🡪 25mL of stock solution + 225mL of MQ. 
100 mM citric acid 1L: 
● Dissolve 21.0 grams of citric acid monohydrate (Sigma #C1909) to 800 mL of MQ 
H20. 
● Add 9.94 grams of NaOH pellets (Merck # 1310 -73-2) 
● Leave on a magnetic stirrer to thoroughly mix 
● Measure the pH and set to 6.0 
● Level the volume up to 1L with MQ H20. 
Note: Use the solution not more than 6 months after preparation 
 
● Preparing 10 L of 1x PBS

ghly mix 
● Measure the pH and set to 6.0 
● Level the volume up to 1L with MQ H20. 
Note: Use the solution not more than 6 months after preparation 
 
● Preparing 10 L of 1x PBS 
o Empty remaining 1x PBS in glass jar by the paper towel dispenser 
o Add 1L of 10x PBS (aliquots located on the left counter by the window) 
o Head to supply room (B303) to collect MQ water with 10L jug 
o Review directions on the tank (double check that resistivit y is 18.2 MΩ) 
o Discard the first 200 mL of MQ before filling jug 
▪ Press button with a beaker symbol to view the volume discharged, adjust volume with 
up and down arrows 
o Press draw button above display screen 
o Shake jug to mix

2 
 
Day 1 (approx. 6.5 h) 
1. Dewaxing and antigen retrieval (approx. 2h 10 min) 
1.1. Baking: place the slides vertically into a grey plastic slide holder and bake in a 560C oven for 
30 min. 
 
1.2. Deparaffinization + rehydration: 
3 x 5 min Xylene 
1 x 3 min 100% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 90% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 70% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 50% ethanol 
1 x 3 min 30% ethanol 
2 x 1 min successive 1x PBS solution 
 
1.3. Antigen retrieval: 
Transfer the slides onto a white plastic holder (looks like a strainer with a long handle) and in 
a glass dish - fill with 250 mL citric acid buffer. 
 
Boil sections in a microwave at 600 W as follows: 
1. Boil for 4 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min. 
2. Boil for 2 min - leave the slides inside for 5 min. 
3. Boil for 2 min. 
4. Let to cool down for 20 mins RT 
Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
2. Pre-staining (approx. 2h) 
2.1. Prepare a moist incubation chamber to minimize evaporation: Wet regular tissue paper 
under the tap water and fold it to fit inside the grey slide box . 
2.2. After cooling and washing from step 1. 3., take out the slides one at a time, dry the 
surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber -free tissue paper . 
2.3. Without letting the tissue dry, quickly add 200 -300 uL odyssey blocking buffer per section 
and incubate for 30 min RT. The use of PAP pen is not needed . Do not spread the blocking

out letting the tissue dry, quickly add 200 -300 uL odyssey blocking buffer per section 
and incubate for 30 min RT. The use of PAP pen is not needed . Do not spread the blocking 
buffer with a pipette t ip, as you might scratch the tissue while doing so . In addition, thorough 
drying of the surroundings of the tissue creates a hydrophobic surface so that the solution 
does not spread outside the tissue. 
2.3.1. During incubation prepare for step 2.5. 
2.4. After the incu bation, remove the blocking buffer by vertically tapping the slide on a tissue 
paper. 
2.5. Next, pre-stain the slides with secondary antibodies and Hoechst only by adding 200 -300uL 
of pre -diluted antibodies per section. Incubate for 60 min at RT in a moist cha mber (from 
2.1). 
 
 Prepare cocktail of secondary antibodies and Hoechst in odyssey blocking buffer: 
 488 goat anti -rabbit 1:1000 (subtract amount of Ab from total vol: 1uL Ab: 999uL buffer) 
 555 goat anti -rat 1:1000 
 647 goat anti -mouse 1:1000 
 HOECHST 1:5000 
 Note: We add non -specific secondary antibodies to reduce autofluorescence

3 
 2.6. Transfer the slides into a vertical grey plastic holder placed in a white plastic container with 
1xPBS. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
 
3. Bleaching/Fluorophore inactivation (approx. 2h) 
3.1. Prepare 50 mL bleaching solution in a falcon tube as follows: 
40 ml 1xPBS 
 7.5ml 30% H2O2 
 2.5ml 480 mM NaOH ( note: reaction begins when NaOH is added ) 
 
3.2. Pour the bleaching solution into a white slide box lid placed on top of a LED light. 
3.3. Using forceps, transfer the slides into a bleaching solution; make sure that the slides are 
comp letely immersed in the solution. Place another LED light on top of the slide box (step 
3.2). Connect bottom and top LED lights to power. 
3.4. Photobleach the sides for 90 min at RT, during which change the buffer solution after every 
30 min for cycles 0 and 1, and for the rest of the cycles change the bleaching solution after 45 
min 
3.5. Wash four ti mes in PBS , ~ 3 min each. 
4. Staining with secondaries and Hoechst (approx. 15 min) 
4.1. During the washing step from 3.4., prepare a cocktail of secondary antibodies and Hoechst in 
odyssey blocking buffer 
488 goat anti -rabbit 1:2000 
 555 goat anti -rat 1:2000 
 647 goat anti -mouse 1:2000 
 HOECHST 1:10 000 
NOTE: Dilutions are different from step 2.2. And this round of secondary antibodies will be 
scanned and used as a negative control 
4.2.

oat anti -mouse 1:2000 
 HOECHST 1:10 000 
NOTE: Dilutions are different from step 2.2. And this round of secondary antibodies will be 
scanned and used as a negative control 
4.2. Add 200-300uL per section and incubate at +4°C o/n in a moist chamber (from 2.1.) 
Day 2 
4.3. Wash the slides three times in 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each 
4.4. Dry the surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber -free tissue paper. Add 100 uL of 
70% glycerol per section and carefully cover with a coverslip, avoiding air bubbles. 
4.5. If air bubbles appear, de -coverslip and try again 
4.6. Scan the slides. 
NOTE: Remove excess glycerol from the surrounding of the slides; the slide holder will 
 become dirty if the slide edges are moist and contain excess glycerol. 
 
5. De-coverslipping 
5.1. Immerse the slides at an angle into the plastic container with 1x PBS, so that the surface with 
the coverslip is facing downward and the slide is orientated with the positive side submerged 
into the plastic container. 
5.2. Leave for about 5 min. Carefully lift the slide out of the cuvette and place into another grey 
slide holder in a white plastic container with 1x PBS.

4 
 NOTE: when lifting the slide, the coverslip should automatically come off. Leave the slides in the 1x 
 PBS for another 5 min if this does not happen. Repeat with a new batch of PBS if needed. 
5.3. Wash three times with RT 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
6. Staining with primary antibodies 
6.1. Prepare wet chamber and place at +4 °C 
6.2. Stain with unconjugated primary antibodies o/n at +4 °C diluted in Odyssey solution. 
Day 3 
7. Staining with secondary antibodies 
7.1. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
7.2. Incubate secondary antibodies 1:2000 and Hoechst 1:10000 in Odyssey buffer for 1 hr RT 
Antibodies Inventory and Antibody Panel Template: Antibodies Inventory Färkkilä lab, UPD 
24 10 2022NC 
UPDATED (June22)_CyCIF antibody inventory_Färkkilä 
Test_Antibodies_22 -02_cycif.xlsx (in LDrive) 
7.3. Wash three times with 1x PBS x3, ~ 3 min each. 
7.4. Dry the surroundings of the tissue section using soft fiber-free tissue paper. Add 100 uL of 
70% glycerol per section and carefully cover with a coverslip, avoiding air bubbles. 
7.5. Scan the slides. 
NOTE: Volume of the 70% glycerol depends on the size of the tissue, bigger tissues may need more 
than 100ul for example 1cm x 1cm tissue might need 120 ul of glycerol 
 
8. Cyclic phase – repeat steps as before 
8.1. Decoverslip using 1x PBS 
8.2. Photobleach for 1.5 h, change the solution after 45 min. 
8.3.

tissue might need 120 ul of glycerol 
 
8. Cyclic phase – repeat steps as before 
8.1. Decoverslip using 1x PBS 
8.2. Photobleach for 1.5 h, change the solution after 45 min. 
8.3. Wash four times in PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
8.4. Stain with pre -conjugated antibodies and Hoechst 1:10000 o/n at +4°C. 
8.5. Wash three times with 1x PBS, ~ 3 min each. 
8.6. Mount with 70% glycerol and scan 
Repeat step 8 for the next round

#### Script to prepare single cell reference to be added to Xenium Web Panel Designer.

install.packages("BiocManager")

library(DropletUtils)
library(biomaRt)
library(Seurat)

sc_OC <- readRDS("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Results/escc_combined.rds")

#verify if values are integers or not (detect previous transformations)
all.equal(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x, as.integer(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x))
head(GetAssayData(object=sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts")@x)

head(rownames(sc_OC))

dplyr::glimpse(GetAssay(sc_OC)@meta.data)

dplyr::glimpse(sc_OC[[]])


# Connect to Ensembl
mart <- useMart("ensembl", dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl")

# Get mapping
gene_map <- getBM(
 attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "hgnc_symbol"),
 filters = "hgnc_symbol",
 values = rownames(sc_OC),
 mart = mart
)

# Merge mapping back to your data
gene_map <- gene_map[gene_map$hgnc_symbol != "", ] # remove empty mappings


# Keep only genes with an Ensembl ID and present in the Seurat object
gene_map <- gene_map[gene_map$ensembl_gene_id != "" & 
 gene_map$hgnc_symbol %in% rownames(sc_OC), ]

# Remove duplicates (if any)
gene_map <- gene_map[!duplicated(gene_map$hgnc_symbol), ]

sc_OC <- subset(sc_OC, features = gene_map$hgnc_symbol)
dim(sc_OC)

# Store original gene symbols in meta.data

(if any)
gene_map <- gene_map[!duplicated(gene_map$hgnc_symbol), ]

sc_OC <- subset(sc_OC, features = gene_map$hgnc_symbol)
dim(sc_OC)

# Store original gene symbols in meta.data
sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$hgnc_symbol <- (gene_map$hgnc_symbol)


# Ensure gene_map is in the same order as the Seurat object
gene_map <- gene_map[match(rownames(sc_OC), gene_map$hgnc_symbol), ]

# Add Ensembl IDs to meta.data in the RNA assay
sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$ensembl_id <- gene_map$ensembl_gene_id

head(sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data)

saveRDS(sc_OC, "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/xenium_scOC.rds")

# Run function
write10xCounts(
 "Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/reference_data_xenium",
 GetAssayData(sc_OC, assay="RNA", layer="counts"),
 gene.id = sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$ensembl_id ,
 gene.symbol = sc_OC[["RNA"]]@meta.data$hgnc_symbol ,
 barcodes = colnames(sc_OC),
 type = "sparse",
 version = "3"
)

# List the files
list.files("Panel/Add_ons/Validations/Datasets/xenium_page_reference/reference_data_xenium")

# Output
#[1] "barcodes.tsv.gz" "features.tsv.gz" "matrix.mtx.gz"

 barcode = barcodes,

import math
import tifffile
import os
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

INPUT_PATH = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/02_Visualization/t-CycIF/Images_IC'
OUTPUT_PATH = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/08_Results/Datasets/09_ML/single_channel_images/'
channels_path = '/home/bonem/NAS_Projects/t-CycIF/t-CycIF_human/01_Registration/channels.txt'

channels = ["Hoechst_1"]

arr = os.listdir(INPUT_PATH)

if __name__ == '__main__':
 print("Image list: {}".format(arr)) # verify you only have the desired images here

 user_input = input("Do you want to proceed? [y/n]")
 if user_input=="y":
 for image in arr:
 if image.startswith('.'):
 print(f"Skipping hidden file: {image}")
 continue
 image_name = image
 sep = '.'
 imageid = image_name.split(sep, 1)[0]
 print("Imageid = {}".format(imageid))
 IMAGE_PATH = os.path.join(INPUT_PATH, image_name)
 print("reading image {}".format(IMAGE_PATH))

 with open(channels_path, 'r') as channel_txt:
 channel_list = [channel.rstrip() for channel in channel_txt]
 if channel_list[0] == "ABS":
 del channel_list[0]
 indexes = []
 for ch in channels: 
 index = channel_list.index(ch)
 indexes.append(index)

 merge = []
 for idx in indexes:
 img = tifffile.imread(IMAGE_PATH, key = idx)
 merge.append(img)

]
 for ch in channels: 
 index = channel_list.index(ch)
 indexes.append(index)

 merge = []
 for idx in indexes:
 img = tifffile.imread(IMAGE_PATH, key = idx)
 merge.append(img)
 
 stack = np.stack(merge, axis=0)

 output_name = OUTPUT_PATH + imageid + ".ome.tif"

 with tifffile.TiffWriter(output_name, bigtiff = True) as tif:
 for img_array in merge:
 tif.write(stack,
 photometric="minisblack",
 metadata={"axes": "CYX"})

 print("Image saved at {}".format(imageid))

R1: INSTRUCTIONS
R2: Make a copy of the template and run the functions from the top button tCycIF MagicGUI
R3: You should give permits for the automation (Google will ask you to authenticate just once)
R4: FOR RESEARCHERS
R5: 📋 Sample_Layout
R6: Panel refers to a specific combination of antibodies used in the full experiment (e.g., A, B, C...).
R7: Versions within each panel indicate the sample split by dilution used (e.g., 1, 2…).
R8: For real experiments (not validations): | → Assign Panel A | → Use Dilution 1 only.
R10: 📋 Panel_Builder
R11: Start by launching the Panel Design Wizard in the t-CyCIF MagicGui.
R12: This links to the most recent version of the Antibody Database
R13: | Use the dropdown or input an antibody ID to auto-fill details.
R14: | Formatting (e.g., colors) will apply a few seconds after selection.
R15: You MUST fill the validation dillution column/s, for version 1 just fill down ValidationDillution1, for v2, ValidationDillution2 ...
R16: | If you are running a real experiment, not a validation,just fill ValidationDillution1 column, you should copy the ValidationDillutionLola for those antibodies that were validated
R17: | ⚠️ IMPORTANT: If an antibody is validated but missing, update the antibody database, not just your local sheet. After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version

antibody is validated but missing, update the antibody database, not just your local sheet. After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version
R18: You MUST fill the cycle number and the panel.
R19: If an antibody vial is missing you will be notified
R20: 📊 Panel_summary
R21: For presentations, publications, or organization, run 📊 Display Panel_summary in tCyCIF MagicGui.
R22: This will display a new tab per each panel, with format, cycle number, marker number in the final image and all kinds of details
R23: This is useful for later computational steps
R25: FOR RESEARCHERS
R26: 🧪 Pipetting_Calculator
R27: You can adjust per-slide volume and Hoechst dilution for automatic calculations.
R28: 🧪 RunPipettingSteps
R29: If you go to the cycif gui and then runpipettingsteps you will get
R30: 🧪 TubePrepAuto | Antibodies + exact volumes to pipet, organized by vial - set of slides
R31: 🧪 ReagentList | List of required antibodies (calculates total volume needed + locations) | | → Useful for verifying inventory and ordering.
R32: 🧪 TubePrepAuto | By default Slide_Number is displaye in Tube prep auto plan.. To change this setting to Unique_Section_ID, go to extensions->App scripts->tCycIF pipetting plan automation->generatePipettingPlan.gs in row 22 Slide_number to Unique_Section_ID.Remember to click the saving disk on top
R33: 🔬 RunScanningGUIs

App scripts->tCycIF pipetting plan automation->generatePipettingPlan.gs in row 22 Slide_number to Unique_Section_ID.Remember to click the saving disk on top
R33: 🔬 RunScanningGUIs
R34: 🔬 Scanning_Plan | It will display the plan for scanning with slideIDs, running numbers and dropdown menus that autoformat
R35: 🔬 Experimental_Assessment | Automatically displays the sheet
R37: FOR ANALYSES
R38: 💻 Display Computational Steps
R39: Lists the samples and the steps needed in the future

Title: Comparative analysis of scRNAseq data from patients with different inherited bone marrow failure syndromes
Pablo Siliceo, Ángel A. de la Cruz, Ulises Juárez, Paula Leal-Anaya, Sara Frías, Alfredo Rodríguez

Introduction: Inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (IBMFS) are a set of heterogeneous genetic diseases caused by germline pathogenic variants in genes involved in hematopoiesis and genomic integrity. Despite sharing common phenotypical manifestations that have aided in opportune diagnosis, such as bone marrow failure, congenital malformations and an increased predisposition to develop cancer, little is known about the shared and distinctive transcriptional programs in their hematopoietic compartment that underlie the clinical manifestations of each disease. Proper patient stratification and characterization of transcriptional regulation behind bone marrow failure development and neoplastic transformation are crucial steps towards dissecting early mechanisms of disease progression and guidance of best treatment regimes.
Objective: Identify transcriptional contingency and perform comparative functional characterization of scRNAseq datasets of hematopoietic stem progenitor cells (HSPC) of patients with IBMFS

Methods: We integrated and analyzed three different publicly available scRNAseq datasets (10x Genomics) of lineage negative, CD34+ cells of bone marrow samples from patients with Fanconi Anemia (FA), Dyskeratosis Congenita (DC) and Diamond-Blackfan Anemia (DBA), as well as healthy controls. By using R package Seurat, we integrated all datasets and performed standard quality control metrics (doublet removal, trimming of cells with high mitochondrial gene percentage and low gene detection and RNA counts) as well as batch effect correction using different methods (Harmony, scANVI, scVI, Seurat and Scanorama). Once integrated, we performed dimensionality reduction (PCA, UMAP), cell clustering and cell type annotation on the combined dataset to dissect the heterogeneity of the data and perform downstream analyses such as differential gene expression (DGE) and gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) through a comparative scheme between patients, syndromes, and healthy controls.

Results: We successfully integrated publicly available datasets of three different sample cohorts from patients with IBMFS: FA, DC and DBA. Through dimensionality reduction, cell clustering and celltype annotation we found distinctive clusters with shared celltypes between syndromes as well as populations with unique transcriptional programs who differed from patient to patient. By means of GSEA and DGE, we found shared enrichment and differential downregulation of genes and pathways related to DNA damage response, metabolic stress and, in some cell clusters, oncogenesis.

Conclusions: Our work provides a basis for an accumulative, comparative and integrated atlas of scRNAseq data from patients with IBMFS. Our results show the integration of datasets from FA, DC and DBA, three major IBMFS. Functional analyses of the combined dataset enabled us to find shared transcriptional programs of cellular stress and oncogenesis between syndromes, and between the celltypes therein contained.

Title: Spatial landscape of Fanconi anemia tumors shows increasing immune exhaustion with carcinogenic progression.
Topic: Cancer

Authors: Pablo Siliceo Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera Mencia3, Fernando Pérez Villatoro3, Angela Szabo3, Ashwini Nagaraj3, Luis A. Flores Mejía1,2, Anna Laury3, Eunike Velleuer4, Anniina Färkkilä5,6, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2

1) Laboratorio de Falla Medular y Carcinogénesis, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 2) Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México. 3) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4) Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld. Germany. 5) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 6) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland.

Introduction. Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by bone marrow failure and an increased cancer predisposition. Advances in bone marrow transplantation (BMT) have reduced the aplastic anemia-related FA mortality; however, there has been an epidemiological shift on the FA morbi-mortality, where patients at a very early age are developing aggressive squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). New methodological avenues in the study of cancer, like the implementation of spatial proteomics with single-cell resolution using formalin-fixed and paraffin-embedded samples (FFPEs) of solid tumors, are paving the way for exciting new therapeutic insights targeting the tumor/immune landscape.

Objective. To study the roleplay of the tumor-immune interactions in FA carcinogenic progression by implementing single-cell spatial proteomics in archived FFPE SCC tumor samples from patients with FA.

Material and methods. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors using highly multiplexed tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) in 11 SCC FFPE tumor samples from patients with FA, with and without previous BMT. A certified pathologist annotated H&E slides of the specimens to identify the carcinogenic progression of the tumors in low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and invasive carcinoma. Slides were prepared from FFPE blocks that were deparaffinized, dehydrated and rehydrated in citrate buffer. Multiple staining cycles with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies were imaged in a Nikon Ni-E scanner. The resulting whole-slide multidimensional images were analyzed in the context of protein expression, cancer stage, immune microenvironment, and spatial tissue architecture using computational analyses based in artificial intelligence and machine learning. The obtained spatial phenotypes of every sample were integrated with the patients’ clinical outcomes and human papillomavirus (HPV) status.

Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing specific immune, tumor and stromal cell compositions. Cellular neighborhoods showed unique spatial arrangements between cell types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increase in T cell infiltration at the invasive carcinoma and formation of immune cell belts at tumor transition zones. Analysis of the expression of functional markers for antigen presentation, interferon activation, cell proliferation, and DNA damage showed particular spatial cell-to-cell interactions that accounted for functional expression patterns, such as an increased expression of oncogenic protein cMyc and a reduced expression of tumor suppressive protein p53 in Ki67+ tumor cells, as well as an increased expression of exhaustion markers, like TIM3 in CD8+ T cells. These unique spatial tissue architecture and expression profiles were also related to the HPV and BMT status of the FA patients.

Conclusions. We identified unique spatial cellular arrangements and cell-to-cell interactions among tumor, immune and stromal cells in FA-SCC tumors, where an increase in CD8+ T cells infiltration with a rather exhausted phenotype and oncogenic expression in tumor cells was shown to correlate with the progression of the disease.

Translational and Clinical Applicability. FA patients, given their underlying DNA repair deficiency, should ideally avoid chemotherapy for treating cancer. The identification of functional immune-tumor interactions can be translated into potential actionable targets and biomarkers for immunotherapy and precision oncology that can benefit FA patients.

Title: Modelling the MYC-p53 network recapitulates cell fate decisions leading to bone marrow failure in FA

Authors: Alfredo Rodríguez1,2, Pablo Siliceo-Portugal1, Daniel Santamaría3, Mara D. Rueda3, Hugo Tovar4, Laura Gómez4, Leda Torres5, Luis Mendoza6, Sara Frías1,5, Eugenio Azpeitia3

MYC and p53 are overexpressed in the hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) from patients with FA. MYC promotes proliferation whereas p53 promotes cell cycle arrest and apoptosis FA HSPCs. Mathematical modeling of th integrating intracellular and extracellular signaling allows the FA bone marrow and understand how FA cells integrate and interpret DNA damage signaling and microenvironmental cues.
Objective: To generate and validate computational models that predict the cell fate of FA HSPCs based on the MYC-p53 regulatory motif.

Methods: Using single cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) datasets from FA HSPCs (Rodríguez et al., Cell Stem Cell, 2021) we inferred MYC. and interactions were incorporated into a discrete Boolean network model that integrates the MYC-p53 intracellular network as well as its interactions with extracellular factors, such as the pro-inflammatory cytokines TGF and TNF. A mathematical model using ordinary differential equation (ODE), which allows levels of activation, was also generated simulat the modulation of factors relevant in the DNA damage response. Combinatorial mutations of the network elements in which the MYC-p53 motif is embedded will be tested using a single-cell CRISPR screening.

Results: HJURP and RLP40 were inferred as MYC and p53 regulators from the FA HSPCs scRNAseq datasetsand incorporated into the MYC-p53 network. Simulations our Boolean network model propose that TGF signaling p53 activation causing cell dead (Figure 1A). On the other hand, survival of FA cells with unrepaired DNA damage w achieved through sustained activation of TNF prompt regulatory motif NF-B and MYC prematurely activate the WIP1 phosphatase, a negative regulator of DNA damage checkpoint proteins (Figure 1B). Modeling of the DNA damage response using ODEs recapitulated the well-known oscillatory behavior of p53 in response to DNA damage his model allowed to simulate the KO of WIP1 and its graded inhibition, which can modify the oscillatory pattern of p53, leading to its stabilization and promotion of apoptosis. Combinatorial mutations of the predicted network elements will be tested using a single-cell CRISPR screening.

Conclusion: In this work we integrated bioinformatic and tools for generating mathematical models that recapitulate FA HSPCs behaviors. Our approach allowed us to predict new components of the MYC-p53 regulatory network and simulate the behavior of FA cells under multiple .

Clinical applicability: Mathematical modeling of the FA regulatory networks has the potential to guide target inference and experimental design. We expect to actionable and new therapeutic opportunities for FA bone marrow failure.

RESULTS
CONCLUSIONS . We are characterizing the TME of SCCs from patients with FA using multidimensional imaging with single -cell spatial 
resolution (Fig. 1 and 2). We observe overall presence of tumor exhaustion markers (Fig. 3), as well as increased immune infiltration 
with tumor progression (Fig. 4), particularly of exhausted T cells (Fig. 5). Macroscale analysis showed that FA tumors exclude immune 
cells (Fig. 6), and neighborhood analysis identified that FA tumors exhaust T cells through a spatially restricted two-tier mechanism, 
mediated by M2 macrophages in the epithelium and dendritic cells in the adjacent stroma (Fig. 7). In our mouse model of FA

carcinogenesis we found increasing numbers of γH2AX+ cells with tumor progression, and arrival of M2 macrophages followed by 
dendritic cells to areas of tumor formation (Fig. 8). Our working model suggests that in FA SCCs T cells interact with exhaustion -
promoting myeloid cells in two compartments, in the malignant epithelium T cells form RCNs with M2 macrophages and in the adjacent 
stroma these T cells form RCNs with dendritic cells, suggesting an active two-tier exhaustion mechanism in these epithelial tumors .

WORKING MODEL
Spatial analysis of human and murine FA tumors shows hypofunctional immune architecture related to cancer progression

Pablo Siliceo-Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera-Mencia3, Luis A. Flores-Mejía1,2, Mahiru Trejo-León1, Fernando Pérez-Villatoro3, Anna Laury3, Renee Fisher4, Michael Epperly4, Aliesha González-Arenas1, Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6,7,8, Joel Greenberger4, Eunike Velleuer9, Anniina Färkkilä3,10,11, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2

Introduction: Clinical manifestations of Fanconi anemia (FA) include bone marrow failure and an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) at an early age. Their underlying DNA repair deficiency makes patients with FA hypersensitive to most conventional DNA-damage-based cancer treatments, stressing the need for alternative therapeutic approaches. The implementation of single-cell spatial technologies in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) have shown promising insights towards the development of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses.
Objective: Identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using single-cell spatial proteomics.

Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis using whole-slide tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) in a total of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of immunofluorescent staining (t-CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies, as well as H&E staining on sequential slides for pathologist annotation of regions of interest (ROI). The resulting cyclic scans were aligned and registered (ASHLAR) into a multidimensional image that was analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO) in the context of their tumor region, carcinogenic progression, tumor-immune architecture, and functional interactions.

Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression (Figure 1A). In human FA SCCs, there was an increase in cellular neighborhoods with DNA damage and an enrichment of tumor-associated macrophages and exhausted T cells along carcinogenic progression (Figure 1B). In the 4NQO carcinogenesis mouse model, changes in the FA mouse epithelium appeared early during treatment (8 weeks), in comparison to wild type mice, including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage (H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells (Figure 1C).

Conclusion: We profiled the TME of both human and mouse FA SCCs through image-based single-cell proteomics and identified unique cellular neighborhoods along carcinogenic progression, where hypofunctional immune infiltration correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocalization with immunosuppressive cell types.

Translational and Clinical Applicability: Through this project we are discovering novel insights accounting for spatial interactions involved in cancer development and immune evasion in FA SCC. Our data has produced clear hypothesis that can further be explored and validated experimentally to develop new immunotherapeutic combinations focusing on patient stratification and improved treatment regimes.

(B) t-CycIF image of hypopharynx tumor from a patient with FA showing the expression of gH2AX and infiltrating immune cells. (C) t-CycIF image of wild type and FA mouse model showing gH2AX, Ki67 and infiltrating immune cells a different times of treatment with 4NQO.
Title: Spatial analysis of Fanconi anemia tumors show hypofunctional immune infiltration and DNA damage

Introduction: Fanconi Anemia (FA) is a genetic syndrome caused by an impaired FA/BRCA DNA repair pathway. Clinical manifestations of the disease include bone marrow failure and an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) at an early age. Their underlying DNA repair deficiency makes patients with FA hypersensitive to most conventional DNA-damage-based cancer treatments, stressing the need for alternative therapeutic approaches. The implementation of single-cell spatial technologies in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) have shown promising insights towards the development of new biomarkers targeting the reactivation of hypofunctional immune responses.
Objective: Identify the underlying functional dynamics driving immune evasion and tumor development in FA SCC by mapping the spatial landscape of the TME using single-cell spatial proteomics.

Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis using whole-slide tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of immunofluorescent staining (t-CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies, as well as H&E staining on sequential slides for pathologist annotation of regions of interest (ROI). The resulting cyclic scans were aligned and registered (ASHLAR) into a multidimensional image that was analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO) in the context of their tumor region, carcinogenic progression, tumor-immune architecture, and functional interactions.

Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression (Figure 1A). In human FA SCCs, there was an increase in cellular neighborhoods with DNA damage and an enrichment of tumor-associated macrophages and exhausted T cells along carcinogenic progression (Figure 1B). In the 4NQO carcinogenesis mouse model, changes in the FA mouse epithelium appeared early (8 weeks), in comparison to wild type mice, including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage (H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells (Figure 1C).

Conclusion: We profiled the TME of both human and mouse FA SCCs through single-cell proteomics and identified unique cellular neighborhoods along carcinogenic progression, where hypofunctional immune infiltration correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocalization with immunosuppressive cell types.

Translational and Clinical Applicability: Through this project we have found novel insights accounting for spatial interactions involved in cancer development and immune evasion in FA SCC. Our data has produced clear hypothesis that can further be explored and validated experimentally to develop new immunotherapeutic combinations focusing on patient stratification and improved treatment regimes.

Spatial analysis of FA tumors identifies conserved topological features associated with immune exhaustion niches
Topic: Cancer

Pablo Siliceo1, Cruz Osuna1, Luis Raul Figueroa Martínez2, Haydeé Peruyero2, Sonia del Marro7, Ada Junquera-Mencia3, Saundarya Shah3, 10Anna Laury3, Renee Fisher4, Michael Epperly4, 9Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6, Ramón García-Escudero7, 10Eunike Velleuer8, Anniina Färkkilä3 , Alfredo Rodríguez1

1Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México. pablosiliceo@iibiomedicas.unam.mx 2Centro de Ciencias Matemáticas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Morelia, Mexico. 3Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States of America. 5Laboratorio de Citogenética, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 6Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 7Molecular and Translational Oncology Unit, Centro de Investigaciones Energéticas, Medioambientales y Tecnológicas (CIEMAT), Avda. Complutense 40, 28040, Madrid, Spain. 8Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 9Instituto Estatal de Cancerología "Dr. Arturo Beltrán Ortega", Guerrero, México. 10Laboratory of Genome Maintenance, Rockefeller University, New York, NY 10065, USA *Corresponding author

Introduction: Fanconi anemia is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an exacerbated risk to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). First-line treatments, like chemotherapy, are commonly avoided in these patients due to their inherent hypertoxicity to DNA-damaging agents, narrowing therapeutic options. The use of spatial omics in the study of the tumor microenvironment (TME) has paved the way for the identification of native interactions and spatial arrangements that could be therapeutically targeted. Spatial data analyses of tissues have leveraged approaches often used in eco-geographical data to decant foundational properties in uncharted landscapes. Methods like neighborhood analysis and topological data analysis, allow for the identification of compositional and structural niches, respectively, underlining complex systems like cancer.
Objective: Identify key spatial features driving immune evasion and tumor development in the TME of FA SCC using spatial omics.

Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed immunofluorescence imaging (t-CycIF) using an immuno-oncological panel (>20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies) in a total of 57 samples from SCC. Samples were obtained from solid tumors of both non-FA and FA patients, as well as chemical (4NQO oral treatment) and spontaneous (Tp53 conditional KO) carcinogenesis models in wild-type and FA mice (Fancd2-/- and Fanca-/-, respectively). H&E of adjacent tissue cuts from each sample were used for pathological annotation (dysplasia, invasive carcinoma and adjacent stroma). Cells were segmented (StarDist) and each marker quantified (MCMICRO) as the median marker intensity within each cell. Finally, data analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods to identify spatial features related to immune suppression and cancer progression. Cellular neighborhood identification (SCIMAP) was used to identify repeating cellular niches, while topological data analysis (), a method grounded in the progressive generation of geometrical networks in space, was used to identify repeating geometrical motifs between relevant cell types

Results: We found phenotypical heterogeneity between sample groups (FA and non-FA) and annotated tumor regions (dysplasia and invasive carcinoma), highlighting a striking recruitment of myeloid cells at the adjacent stroma of tumor regions with increased damage (H2AX) and proliferation (Ki67) signals in FA samples (both in human and murine samples). Neighborhood analysis identified cellular niches of dendritic cells, Tregs and M2 macrophages co-occurring with exhausted (TIM3+ and PD-1+) Tcells in FA samples, showing differential interactions in the stroma and epithelium. On the other hand, found global topological footprints differentiating between early dysplasia and invasive carcinoma, while also detecting fine-grained neighborhood-specific geometrical features associated with previously found myeloid-mediated Tcell exhaustion.

Conclusion: We have characterized through spatial proteomics the TME of human and murine FA SCCs, as well as non-FA SCCs, and identified unique FA-specific topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression.
translationalThis project provides a hypothesis-generating of the TME of FA SCCs, focusing on patient stratification and the exploration of novel immunotherapeutic approaches to treat patients.

Título: Arquitectura inmuno-espacial de tumores Anemia de Fanconi

Autores: Pablo Siliceo-Portugal, Ada Junquera-Mencia, Luis A. Flores-Mejía, Mahiru Trejo-León, Fernando Pérez-Villatoro, Anna Laury, Aliesha González-Arenas, Sara Frías, Joel Greenberger, Eunike Velleuer, Anniina Färkkilä, Alfredo Rodríguez

Introducción: La Anemia de Fanconi (AF) es un síndrome de inestabilidad cromosómica carcinomas de células escamosas (CCE). Nuevas estrategias metodológicas en el estudio del cancer, como la protéomica espacial con resolución unicelular, sobre el microambiente tumoral (MAT) y de tratamientos alternativos a la qumioterapia (tratamientopacientes con AF).

Metodología: Utilizando inmunofluorescencia cíclica múltiple en téjidos (t-CycIF), caracterizamos el microambiente tumoral (MAT) de 33 muestras de CCE de pacientes con y sin AF, de ratones tratados oralmente durante 20 semanas con el potente carcinogénico 4NQO. Al escanear secuencialmente las muestras con microscopio de alta resolución RareCyte CyteFinder II, se obtuvo la expresión protéica del tejido a través de imágenes multidimensionales fueron analizadas en el contexto de su progresión carcinogénica, arquitectura inmuno-tumoral, e funcional, utilizando diferentes métodos computacionales (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO).

Resultados: En muestras de pacientes AF identificamos heterogeneidad fenotípica la progresión un incremento en el vecindario espacial conformado por células T anérgicas (CD8+TIM3) y macrófagos protumorales tipo M2 (CD163+). n el modelo de ratón AF encontramos una correlación entre la presencia de células proliferantes con daño en el ADN (Ki67+H2AX+) y uninfiltración inmune comparación los controles.

Conclusión: arreglos inmuno-tumorales representativos de la progresión de CCE de pacientes con AF y un modelo de ratón. una incrementada infiltración inmune hipofuncional que correlaciona con la presencia de macrófagos protumorales en humano, y células con daño en el ADN en ratón.
Título: Arquitectura inmuno-espacial de tumores Anemia de Fanconi

Introducción: La Anemia de Fanconi (AF) es un síndrome de inestabilidad cromosómica carcinomas de células escamosas (CCE). Nuevas estrategias metodológicas en el estudio del cancer, como la protéomica espacial con resolución unicelular, sobre el microambiente tumoral (MAT) y de tratamientos alternativos a la qumioterapia (tratamientopacientes con AF).

Metodología: Utilizando inmunofluorescencia cíclica múltiple en téjidos (t-CycIF), caracterizamos el microambiente tumoral (MAT) de 33 muestras de CCE de pacientes con y sin AF, de ratones tratados oralmente durante 20 semanas con el potente carcinogénico 4NQO. Al escanear secuencialmente las muestras con microscopio de alta resolución RareCyte CyteFinder II, se obtuvo la expresión protéica del tejido a través de imágenes multidimensionales fueron analizadas en el contexto de su progresión carcinogénica, arquitectura inmuno-tumoral, e funcional, utilizando diferentes métodos computacionales (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO).

Resultados: En muestras de pacientes AF identificamos heterogeneidad fenotípica la progresión un incremento en el vecindario espacial conformado por células T anérgicas (CD8+TIM3) y macrófagos protumorales tipo M2 (CD163+). n el modelo de ratón AF encontramos una correlación entre la presencia de células proliferantes con daño en el ADN (Ki67+H2AX+) y uninfiltración inmune comparación los controles.

Conclusión: arreglos inmuno-tumorales representativos de la progresión de CCE de pacientes con AF y un modelo de ratón. una incrementada infiltración inmune hipofuncional que correlaciona con la presencia de macrófagos protumorales en humano, y células con daño en el ADN en ratón.

Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an increased cancer predisposition. Being fundamentally a DNA repair deficiency, FA makes patients hypersensitive to DNA damaging agents, most of which are used in conventional cancer therapies, urging the need for new treatments. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors using highly multiplexed tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) in 11 SCC FFPE tumor samples from patients with FA. The resulting whole-slide multidimensional images were analyzed in the context of protein expression, cancer stage, immune microenvironment, and spatial tissue architecture using computational analyses based in artificial intelligence and machine learning. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing specific immune, tumor and stromal cell compositions. Cellular neighborhoods showed unique spatial arrangements between cell types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increase in T cell infiltration at the invasive carcinoma and formation of immune cell belts at tumor transition zones.

types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increase in T cell infiltration at the invasive carcinoma and formation of immune cell belts at tumor transition zones. Analysis of the expression of functional markers for antigen presentation, interferon activation, cell proliferation, and DNA damage showed particular spatial cell-to-cell interactions that accounted for functional expression patterns, such as the expression of the exhaustion marker TIM3 in T cells neighboring pro-tumoral M2 macrophages.

vector_chunks.text: Fanconi anemia (FA) is a genetic syndrome characterized by an exacerbated predisposition to develop squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of 15 SCC samples from FA tumors using highly multiplexed tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF). We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among tumor regions, showing unique spatial arrangements between cell types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increased infiltration of T cells at tumor transition zones. Cellular neighborhood analysis, accounting for recurrent cell-to-cell interactions, showed an increased co-localization of exhausted T cells (TIM3+) with M2 macrophages (pro-tumoral) at the invasive carcinoma, compared to tissue dysplasia.

Pablo Siliceo 
 Topological data analysis identifies conserved multiscale geometrical features associated with immune exhaustion in squamous cell carcinomas Pablo Siliceo1, Cruz Osuna1, Luis Raul Figueroa Martínez2, Haydeé Peruyero2, Sonia del Marro6, Eugenio Azpeitia2, Azucena Ocampo9, Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6, Ramón García-Escudero6, Joel Greenberger4, Eunike Velleuer8, Anniina Färkkilä3, Alfredo Rodríguez1* 1Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México. pablosiliceo@iibiomedicas.unam.mx 2Centro de Ciencias Matemáticas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Morelia, Mexico. 3Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States of America. 5Laboratorio de Citogenética, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 6Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 7Molecular and Translational Oncology Unit, Centro de Investigaciones Energéticas, Medioambientales y Tecnológicas (CIEMAT), Avda. Complutense 40, 28040, Madrid, Spain. 8Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 9Instituto Estatal de Cancerología "Dr. Arturo Beltrán Ortega", Guerrero, México.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/HCA General Meeting 2025 (Singapur)/25GM Abstract Pablo Siliceo.pdf
vector_chunks.text: , Madrid, Spain. 8Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 9Instituto Estatal de Cancerología "Dr. Arturo Beltrán Ortega", Guerrero, México. *Corresponding author Abstract Spatial omics have paved the way for the identification of native targetable interactions in the tumour microenvironment (TME) by generating complex multidimensional tissue atlases. However, data interpretation heavily relies on the definition of cellular niches through cell type proximity and composition, ignoring key dimensions of tissue architecture, like the geometrical organization of cells. The use of topological methods in biological datasets has enabled non-supervised identification of higher-dimensional features that more accurately describe and predict data shape and connectivity. A fine example is the implementation of topological data analysis (TDA) by Rips complex formation, a method grounded in the progressive generation of geometrical networks in space, to identify repeating structural motifs in data. Nonetheless, its application in spatial biology is rather scant. We implemented neighbourhood analysis and TDA in data from multiplexed immunofluorescent whole-section images of squamous cell carcinomas from patients, as well as chemical (4NQO) and spontaneous (conditional p53 KO) carcinogenesis mouse models.

Unlike neighbourhood analysis alone, we found niche-specific topological footprints encoding for multiscale geometrical features associated with disease progression and myeloid-mediated T cell exhaustion. In this work we demonstrate how TDA is a useful tool for the exploration, visualization and analysis of spatial networks in the TME.
Title: Exhausted T cells and M2 macrophages form progression-related spatial arrangements in tumors of patients with cancer-prone syndrome Fanconi Anemia.
Keywords: Cancer, Fanconi anemia, tumor microenvironment, spatial proteomics

Authors: Pablo Siliceo Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera Mencia3, Fernando Pérez Villatoro3, Angela Szabo3, Ashwini Nagaraj3, Luis A. Flores Mejía1,2, Anna Laury3, Eunike Velleuer4, Anniina Färkkilä5,6, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2

1) Laboratorio de Falla Medular y Carcinogénesis, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 2) Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México. 3) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4) Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld. Germany. 5) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 6) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland.

Introduction. Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by increased cancer predisposition, particularly to the development of aggressive squamous cell carcinomas (SCC) at a very early age. New methodological avenues in the study of cancer, like the implementation of spatial proteomics with single-cell resolution using formalin-fixed and paraffin-embedded samples (FFPEs) of solid tumors, are paving the way for exciting new therapeutic insights targeting the tumor/immune landscape.

Material and methods. We performed single-cell spatial profiling of the tumor-immune microenvironment of FA tumors using highly multiplexed tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) in 11 SCC FFPE tumor samples from patients with FA. A certified pathologist annotated H&E slides of the specimens to identify the carcinogenic progression of the tumors in low-grade dysplasia, high-grade dysplasia, and invasive carcinoma. FFPE blocks were deparaffinized, dehydrated and rehydrated in citrate buffer. Slides underwent multiple staining cycles with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies and were later imaged in each staining cycle with a Nikon Ni-E scanner. The resulting whole-slide multidimensional images were analyzed in the context of protein expression, cancer stage, immune microenvironment, and spatial tissue architecture using computational analyses based in artificial intelligence and machine learning (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO).

Results. We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing specific immune, tumor and stromal cell compositions. Cellular distribution showed unique spatial arrangements between cell types that correlated with carcinogenic progression, like an increased infiltration of T cells* at the invasive carcinoma compared with low- and high-grade dysplasia, as well as the formation of immune cell belts at tumor transition zones. Cellular neighborhood analysis, accounting for recurrent cell-to-cell interactions, showed that at the invasive carcinoma stage there is increased co-localization of exhausted T cells (TIM3+) with pro-tumoral M2 macrophages*, and loss of PD1 expression*, *(p<0.001).
Conclusions.

We found progression-related spatial arrangements of cells in the tumors, with a predominant cellular neighborhood composed of exhausted T cells and pro-tumoral M2 macrophages at the invasive carcinoma.

Further identification of functional tumor-immune interactions among cell types could later be translated into potential actionable targets and biomarkers for immunotherapy and precision oncology for patients with rare cancer-prone syndromes.

vector_chunks.text: Spatial analysis of homologous recombination-deficient tumors identifies myeloid-mediated topological networks associated with immune exhaustion

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Introduction: Homologous recombination (HR) is a fundamental DNA repair pathway in charge of maintaining genome stability. HR deficiency (HRD) is a driver of tumorigenesis and often indicative of sensitivity to targeted therapy with PARP inhibitors, making its detection, germline or somatic, of high clinical importance. Nonetheless, treatment resistance has been recognized as a major challenge in the clinic and little is known about the immunogenetic landscape underlying the tumor microenvironment (TME) and its effect on tumor progression.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Objective: Identify spatial patterns driving immune evasion and tumor development in the TME of HRD tumors using spatial omics.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed immunofluorescence imaging (t-CycIF) in 33 samples from squamous cell carcinomas from patients with and without pathognomonic germline HRD syndrome, Fanconi anemia (FA) and non-FA, respectively. H&E of adjacent tissue cuts from each sample were used to map histopathological annotations in the images acquired (LGD: low grade dysplasia, HGD: high grade dysplasia, CIS: carcinoma in-situ, IC: invasive carcinoma). Cells were segmented (StarDist) and each marker quantified (MCMICRO) as the median marker intensity within each cell. Finally, single-cell expression matrixes were analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods to identify spatial features related to immune suppression and cancer progression. Cellular neighborhood identification (SCIMAP) was used to identify repeating cellular niches, while topological data analysis (TDA), a method that identifies repeating geometrical motifs in space, was used to identify topological footprints underlying spatial arrangements of relevant cell types across histopathological progression.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Results We observed marked phenotypic heterogeneity between FA and non-FA sample groups, as well as across annotated tumor regions. FA tumors (HRD) showed reduced lymphocyte recruitment at early stages (LGD and HGD), followed by progressive myeloid enrichment at later stages (CIS and IC) in both epithelial and stromal compartments.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Neighborhood analysis identified that FA tumors had a progressive increase in stroma-restricted myeloid niches that correlated with TIM3-mediated T cell exhaustion, contrasting with canonical PD-1-associated exhaustion in non-FA samples (HRP). Finally, TDA found stable topological footprints scaffolding myeloid networks at immune exhaustion hotspots, suggesting an HRD-specific spatial organization of myeloid cells that contributes to impaired antitumoral responses.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Biology WCS (Londres)/Abstract/Abstract.docx
vector_chunks.text: Conclusion: We characterized the tumor microenvironment of HRD tumors using spatial proteomics and identified distinct topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression. This project provides a hypothesis-generating cellular and topological atlas of the TME of HRD tumors, focusing on patient stratification and alternative targeted therapies against exploring the inhibition of TIM3 ligands.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.docx
vector_chunks.text: Introduction: Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an increased predisposition to develop aggressive squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). Due to its underlying DNA repair deficiency, FA has been used as de facto model to study carcinogenesis and identify early mechanisms of tumor initiation and development.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.docx
vector_chunks.text: Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed single-cell spatial analysis using whole-slide tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non-FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of immunofluorescent staining (t-CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome-tagged antibodies.). The resulting cyclic scans were aligned and registered (ASHLAR) into a multidimensional image that was analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, MCMICRO) in the context of their tumor region, carcinogenic progression, tumor-immune architecture, and functional interactions.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.docx
vector_chunks.text: Result: We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression. In FA patients, there was an increase in cellular neighborhoods with DNA damage and an enrichment of tumor-associated macrophages and exhausted T cells along carcinogenic progression. In the 4NQO carcinogenesis mouse model, changes in the FA mouse epithelium appeared early (8 weeks), in comparison to wild type mice, including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage (H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.docx
vector_chunks.text: Conclusion: We profiled the TME of FA SCCs through image-based single-cell proteomics and identified unique cellular neighborhoods along carcinogenic progression, where hypofunctional immune infiltration correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocalization with immunosuppressive cell types.

1.Introducción La anemia de Fanconi (FA) es un síndrome de inestabilidad genómica causado por alteraciones genéticas en la vía de reparación de ADN, FA/BRCA . Dentro 
del fenotipo clínico destaca una incrementada predisposición a desarrollar carcinomas de células escamosas (CCE) durante el inicio de la adultez (Fig. 1A y 1B). Al tener una 
deficiencia en la reparación del ADN, los pacientes son hipersensibles a tratamientos clastogénicos como la quimioterapia . Nuevas tecnologías en el estudio del cáncer, como
laproteómica espacial unicelular, permiten eldesarrollo deterapias alternativas basadas enelestudio deinteracciones funcionales dentro delmicroambiente tumoral (TAM) .
2. Objetivo Estudiar la arquitectura espacial del 
MAT de CCE de pacientes con AF con el fin de 
identificar arreglos espaciales e interacciones 
funcionales con valor inmunoterapéutico .Contacto:
psiliceop@gmail.com
3. Metodología Realizamos un análisis espacial multiplex utilizando inmunofluorescencia cíclica múltiple entejido (t-CycIF) 
(Fig. 2A) en 33 cortes de tejido en laminillas obtenidos a partir de bloques de tumores embebidos en formalina y fijados en

munofluorescencia cíclica múltiple entejido (t-CycIF) 
(Fig. 2A) en 33 cortes de tejido en laminillas obtenidos a partir de bloques de tumores embebidos en formalina y fijados en 
parafina (FFPE) demuestras deCCE depacientes con ysinAF(Fig.1B),yderatones AF(Fancd 2-/-)ysilvestres tratados
oralmente durante 20semanas con elpotente carcinogénico 4NQO (Fig. 1C). Implementamos multiples ciclos de tinción 
inmunofluorescente (t-CycIF) con un panel de >20 anticuerpos acoplados a fluorocromos (Tabla 1), fotografiado (Rarecyte 
Finder II) e inactivación de fluoróforos, además de tinciones de H&E en cortes adjacentes para anotar regiones de la 
progression (Fig. 2B). Las imagenes resultantes de cada ciclo fueron computacionalmente unidas en una imagen 
multidimensional por muestra (Fig. 2C) en donde se realizó una segmentación del área de cada célula en la imagen basado en 
la señal de la tinción del ADN (Hoechst) (Fig. 2D). Cada célula fue clasificada con base en su perfil de expresión de acuerdo a su 
identidad (inmune , tumoral o stromal) y su fenotipo functional (Fig. 2E). Finalmente, se hizo una análisis espacial para la 
identificación de vecindarios celulares recurrentes (RCN) a partir de la composición espacial alrededor de cada célula (Fig. 2F). 
Los RCN luego fueron graficados como polígonos en la imagen para su visualización (Fig. 2G).

vector_chunks.text:
Title: Exhausted T cells and M2 macrophages form progression -related spatial arrangements in tumors 
of patients with cancer -prone syndrome Fanconi Anemia . 
Pablo Siliceo -Portugal1,2, Ada Junquera -Mencia3, Luis A. Flores -Mejía1,2, Mahiru Trejo -León1, Fernando 
Pérez -Villatoro3, Anna Laury3, Renee Fisher4, Michael Epperly4, Aliesha González -Arenas1, Sara Frías1,5, 
Leonard Harris6,7,8, Joel Greenberger4, Eunike Velleuer9, Anniina Färkkilä3,10,11, Alfredo Rodríguez1,2 
1) Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Aut ónoma de México, México. 2) 
Laboratorio de Falla Medular y Carcinogénesis, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 3) Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, 
Finland. 4) University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States of America. 5) Laboratorio de Ci togenética, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, 
México. 6) Department of B iomedical Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 7) Interdisciplinary Graduate Program i n Cell and 
Molecular Biology, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 8) Cancer Biology Program, Winthrop P Rockefeller Cancer Institute, University of 
Arkansas for Medical Sciences, Little Rock, Arkansas, United States.

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
le, Arkansas, United States. 8) Cancer Biology Program, Winthrop P Rockefeller Cancer Institute, University of 
Arkansas for Medical Sciences, Little Rock, Arkansas, United States. 9) Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinik um, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 10) 
Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki Unive rsity Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 11) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland. 
Introduction : Fanconi anemia (FA) is a chromosome instability syndrome characterized by an increased 
predisposition to develop aggressive squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). Due to its underlying DNA repair 
deficiency, FA has been used as de facto model to study carcinogenesis and identify early mechanisms of tumor 
initiation and development. 
Materials and methods : We performed multiplexed single -cell spatial analysis using whole -slide tissue cyclic 
immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) of 33 tissue slides cut from formalin -fixed paraffin -embedded (FFPE) 
anogenital and head and neck SCC tumor samples from both non -FA and FA patients, as well as wild type and 
FA mice treated orally with the potent carcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of 
immunofluorescent staining (t -CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome -
tagged antibodies .).

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
rcinogen 4NQO. Slides underwent multiple cycles of 
immunofluorescent staining (t -CyCIF) and scanning (Rarecyte Finder II) with a panel of >20 fluorochrome -
tagged antibodies .). The resulting cyclic scans wer e aligned and registered (ASHLAR) into a multidimensional 
image that was analyzed using state -of-the-art computational methods (SCIMAP, Napari, StarDist, 
MCMICRO) in the context of their tumor region, carcinogenic progression, tumor -immune architecture, an d 
functional interactions. 
Result : We identified phenotypical heterogeneity among the annotated tumor regions, showing increased 
diversity of cellular neighborhoods that correlated with carcinogenic progression. In FA patients, there was 
an increase in cellular neighborhoods with DNA dama ge and an enrichment of tumor -associated 
macrophages and exhausted T cells along carcinogenic progression. In the 4NQO carcinogenesis mouse 
model, changes in the FA mouse epithelium appeared early (8 weeks), in comparison to wild type mice, 
including extensive fields of cells with DNA damage ( H2AX) that expressed proliferation markers (Ki67), and 
infiltration of immune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells 
Conclusion : We profiled the TME of FA SCCs through image -based single -cell proteomics and identified

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Aplicación/Abstract_Siliceo.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
mmune cells, which are in close contact with DNA damaged epithelial cells 
Conclusion : We profiled the TME of FA SCCs through image -based single -cell proteomics and identified 
unique cellular neighborhoods along carcinogenic progression, where hypofunctional immune infiltration 
correlated with DNA damage presence in tumor cells and colocal ization with immunosuppressive cell 
types.

Attendee,
This
event
was

by

Institute
(INCA)
in

with
Wellcome
Connecting
Science
(WCS).

to

and

and
the

of

to

Over
the

genomics
has
witnessed
rapid

fundamentally
altering
how
we
approach
and
resolve
biological
inquiries.
The
ability
to
examine
individual
cells
has
unveiled
gene
expression
dynamics
and
cellular
diversity
previously
obscured
in
aggregate
analysis.
However,
with
the

profiling
of
millions
of
cells,
challenges
arise
in
experimental
design,
mitigating
errors
and
biases,
and
handling
extensive
datasets.
Nevertheless,
this
scenario
is
more
demanding
in

America
(LATAM)
countries,
which
face
challenges
due
to
limited
infrastructure,
funding
constraints,
and
a
shortage
of
expertise
hindering
the
adoption
of
these
advanced
technologies.
To
overcome
the
challenges
in
LATAM
and
promote
a
collaborative
approach
between
countries,
the

Cell
LATAM
Symposium
aims
to
bring
together
local
researchers
interested
in
single-cell
technologies
to
discuss
the
latest
key

vector_chunks.text:
Unraveling
The
Plasticity
Of
Differentiated
Mesenchymal
Stromal
Cells
Villa
Sifontes,
Alejandro
David
1
1Unidad
de
Terapia
Celular
-
Laboratorio
de
Patología
Celular
y
Molecular,
Centro
de 
Medicina
Regenerativa,
Instituto
Venezolano
de
Investigaciones
Científicas
(IVIC),
Apartado 
21827,
Caracas
1020-A,
Venezuela.
It
has
been
documented
that
when
differentiated
in
other
lineages
(such
as
osteogenic
and 
adipogenic),
mesenchymal
stromal
cells
(MSCs)
appear
to
retain
their
immunoregulatory 
ability.
That
may
be
in
part
due
to
the
heterogeneity
in
MSCs
cultures,
in
which
not
all
the 
cells
may
be
able
to
differentiate,
therefore
maintain
their
immunomodulatory
properties. 
Nonetheless,
it
may
be
possible
that
these
differentiated
cells
may
express
a
mixed 
phenotype
that
allows
them
to
retain
some
degree
of
MSCs
function.
This
notion
is 
supported
by
the
fact
that
MSCs
have
been
found
to
exhibit
differential
expression
profiles 
depending
on
their
tissue
location,
even
within
the
same
donor.
In
relation
to
the
above,
the 
use
of
single
cell
sequencing
(SCS)
technology
could
aid
in
the
identification
of 
subpopulations
of
MSCs
not
only
prone
to
differentiate
into
some
particular
cellular
subtype, 
but
also
prone
to
exert
a
greater
immunoregulatory
effect
and
lower
probability
of
rejection. 
By
isolating
human
bone
marrow-derived
MSCs,
the
most
frequently
used
source
of
MSCs 
for

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Simposio/SingleCellSymposium2024_abstract_book-2.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
lso
prone
to
exert
a
greater
immunoregulatory
effect
and
lower
probability
of
rejection. 
By
isolating
human
bone
marrow-derived
MSCs,
the
most
frequently
used
source
of
MSCs 
for
clinical
trials,
followed
by
the
isolation
of
single
cells,
subsequent
DNA/RNA
extraction 
from
individual
cells
can
be
performed
in
order
to
amplify
the
genetic
material
and
construct 
a
DNA
and/or
RNA
library
for
subsequent
high-throughput
sequencing,
after
which
the
data 
can
be
analyzed.
Just
as
there
are
functionally
distinct
subpopulations
of
undifferentiated 
MSCs,
since
SCS
analysis
has
revealed
that
even
cells
from
the
same
tissue
source
and 
donor
comprise
different
clusters
or
cells
subpopulations
with
different
biological
functions 
(such
as
stemness,
functionality
and
proliferation),
it
is
to
be
expected
that
after
SCS 
analysis,
functionally
distinct
populations
of
differentiated
MSCs
with
different 
immunoregulatory,
functional
and
proliferative
capacity
can
be
identified.
If
so,
further 
analysis
of
the
secretome
of
these
subpopulations
as
well
as
their
epigenetic
profile
could 
help
to
elucidate
the
factors
that
regulate
this
cellular
heterogeneity.
8

Understanding
Neuroblastoma
Through
The
Integration
Of
Single
Cell
Data
Federico
J.
Garde
1,
Candela
D.
Pastore1,
Daniela
J.
Di
Bella2,
Abel
L.
Carcagno1
1
Cell
Differentiation
and
Cancer
Laboratory,
ETI1,
IQUIBICEN-CONICET,
FCEyN-UBA, 
CABA,
Argentina.
2
Department
of
Stem
Cell
and
Regenerative
Biology,
Harvard
University, 
Boston,
USA.
Neuroblastoma
(NB)
is
the
most
common
extracranial
childhood
tumor
arising
from
the 
adrenal
medulla.
NB
is
a
tumor
derived
from
neural
crest
cells
and
is
often
considered
a 
disease
associated
with
defective
cell
differentiation.
The
generation
of
reference
maps
of 
primary
NB
tumors
and
the
analysis
of
undifferentiated
neural
crest-derived
cells
present
in 
the
embryonic
adrenal
medulla
by
scRNAseq
are
invaluable
tools
for
deciphering
the 
heterogeneity
and
origin
of
these
tumors.
Our
aims
were:
1)
Generate
and
analyze
a
unique 
reference
map
of
neuroblastoma
tumors
by
integrating
publicly
available
single-cell
RNA 
sequencing
data.
2)
Use
this
dataset
to
evaluate
ASCL1
as
a
potential
target
for
NB
therapy 
as
a
complement
of
our
in
vitro
results
showing
that
ASCL1
silencing
in
NB
induces
cellular 
differentiation
and
results
in
impaired
tumor
aggressiveness
We
integrated
data
from 
Kildisiute
et
al,
Dong
et
al,
and
Jansky
et
al.
using
Seurat-CCA
integration
and
the
resulting 
dataset
was
filtered
to
obtain
the
tumoral
cells
previously

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Simposio/SingleCellSymposium2024_abstract_book-2.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
e
integrated
data
from 
Kildisiute
et
al,
Dong
et
al,
and
Jansky
et
al.
using
Seurat-CCA
integration
and
the
resulting 
dataset
was
filtered
to
obtain
the
tumoral
cells
previously
identified
by
inferCNV. 
Dimensionality
reduction
was
performed
on
the
obtained
dataset
and
clusters
were 
calculated
by
Uniform
manifold
approximation
and
projection
(UMAP).
Examining
the 
expression
of
gene
signatures
specific
to
human
fetal
adrenal
medulla
cell
types
and 
aligning
our
dataset
with
scRNAseq
data
from
the
same
organ
using
Seurat
TransferID,
we 
found
that
NB
cells
share
a
common
origin,
adrenal
medulla`
sympathoblasts
and
that 
heterogeneity
can
be
explained
by
their
grade
of
cellular
differentiation
and
associated
cell 
cycle.
This
was
supported
by
the
differentially
enriched
processes
and
pathways 
characterized
by
Gene
Ontology
and
Reactome
analyses
and
by
trajectory
analysis 
performed
by
combining
CytoTrace
potency
score
with
Monocle3.
Finally,
we
found
that 
ASCL1
was
mainly
expressed
in
less
differentiated
cells,
and
non-negative
matrix 
factorization
revealed
its
co-expression
with
genes
linked
to
early
neurogenesis,
cell 
migration,
and
proliferation.
9

vector_chunks.text:
Single-Cell
Atlas
Reveals
Immunophenotypes
Associated
With
Clinical
Outcome
In 
Pancreatic
Cancer
Gabriel
F.
Pozo
de
Mattos
Pereira
1,
Marvin
Paulo
Lins1,
Alessandro
Bersch
Osvaldt2, 
Simone
Marcia
dos
Santos
Machado2,
Cristina
Cazabuena
Bonorino1,
Eduardo 
Cremomese
Filippi-Chiela2,3
1
Universidade
Federal
de
Ciências
da
Saúde
de
Porto
Alegre
(UFCSPA).
2
Hospital
de 
Clínicas
de
Porto
Alegre
(HCPA).
3
Universidade
Federal
do
Rio
Grande
do
Sul
(UFRGS)
Pancreatic
ductal
adenocarcinoma
(PDAC)
is
a
highly
aggressive
and
lethal
tumor.
PDAC 
also
shows
poor
response
to
many
conventional
treatments
and
is
refractory
to 
immunotherapy.
We
hypothesize
that
these
challenges
arise
from
the
highly
heterogeneous 
and
immunosuppressive
tumor
microenvironment
(TME)
in
PDAC.
Therefore,
to 
comprehend
the
players
behind
this
immunosuppressive
TME,
we
integrated
10
publicly 
available
datasets
into
a
robust
PDAC
scRNA
atlas
covering
201
patients
(159
PDAC,
24 
non-cancer,
and
18
metastatic
PDAC).
The
TME
showed
distinct
composition
for
each 
tissue,
predominating
a
fibro-inflammatory
phenotype
with
infiltrating
Tregs
and
dysfunctional 
CD8
T
cells
in
primary
PDACs.
In
contrast,
adjacent
normal
tissue
exhibited
enrichment
of 
memory
CD8
T
cells
(FDR
<
0.1).
Metastatic
PDAC
was
characterized
by
a
reduction
in 
stromal
cells
combined
with
myeloid
and
naive
T
cells
infiltration.
Comparison

vector_chunks.source_file: 3_FA/5. Writing & dissemination/Conference material/Single Cell Genomics 2024 (Río de Janeiro)/Simposio/SingleCellSymposium2024_abstract_book-2.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
enrichment
of 
memory
CD8
T
cells
(FDR
<
0.1).
Metastatic
PDAC
was
characterized
by
a
reduction
in 
stromal
cells
combined
with
myeloid
and
naive
T
cells
infiltration.
Comparison
between 
treated
and
untreated
PDAC
revealed
the
enrichment
of
cancer-associated
fibroblasts 
(CAFs)
in
treated
patients.
Besides,
T
cells
from
treated
patients
exhibited
higher
levels
of 
heat
shock
genes
which
has
been
linked
to
immunotherapy
resistance.
Next,
we
stratified 
patients
based
on
TME
cell
composition.
Unsupervised
clustering
revealed
6
clusters: 
Desert
(high
tumor
cell
fraction);
Fibrotic
(high
fraction
of
stromal
cells);
Normal-like
(high 
fraction
of
acinar
and
normal
ductal
cells);
M-enriched
(high
fraction
of
myeloid
cells), 
Immune-CD8_high
and
Immune-CD8_low.
The
Immune-CD8_high
cluster
was
associated 
with
a
good
prognosis
in
a
dataset
(n=43)
present
in
our
atlas.
Then,
we
performed
bulk 
RNA
deconvolution
of
the
TCGA
cohort
(n=145)
and
recovered
almost
all
(except
for 
M-enriched)
previously
identified
clusters.
Survival
analysis
showed
better
overall
survival
for 
the
Immune-CD8_high
cluster
(
p
<
0.05).
Collectively,
our
study
identifies
distinct
features
of 
immune
escape
in
PDAC
associated
with
response
to
therapy
and
patient
survival, 
reinforcing
the
need
for
better
patient
stratification.
10
